title: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
wikitext:
[]: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Tue Mar 18, 2025 6:47 pm I wouldn't call a character who masturbates over a comatose Asuka someone with a healthy respect for women, just throwing it out there. Why is it some people on here and Reddit seem to balk at Shinji being misogynistic or analyzing his behavior through that lens? Even outside of the masturbation scene, we have moments such as Shinji attempting to kiss a sleeping Asuka (showing he disregarded her boundaries longer than EoE), or his visions of Rei, Misato and Asuka while he was in Unit 01 and Instrumentality, or how EoE has Asuka's rejecting him literally cause the end of the world. These moments particularly with how he seems to get angry over Misato having sex with Kaji in either version of Instrumentality, suggest he has a hard time seeing women as people. Sure I feel some Tumblr analysis overemphasize particular moments- his comment in Ep. 1 about Misato being immature in particular is clearly a joke at her expense for stealing batteries- but I wouldn't say it's unwarrented. EoE stages Shinji's attempted murder of Asuka like a domestic abuse scene for a reason, and I think it adds a dimension to his character and the themes of the series. Shinji's biggest problems come from his self-loathing, how he tries to use different things as a replacement for a sense of self, such as being a pilot or winning his father's love, which leads to him not understanding himself or other people. The misogyny, in my opinion, is the ugliest representation of Shinji's self-hate leading to a lack of understanding, it's the way he most resembles a stereotypical anime audience member/nerd/whatever you wanna put in here. He's not a one dimensional woman hater, he has legitimate problems that would bother most people, he tries to help at times despite himself, but in addition to being blind to how people don't immediately hate him- he never tried to reconcile with Toji even though Toji probably wouldn't think he was responsible- he has difficulty reconciling the complexities in the women in his life even though he wants them to be a source of comfort. Misato, dressed as a stereotypical mother figure in that sandbox scene, has a sexual side that angers Shinji for reasons beyond just not wanting to see her fuck Kaji. The climax of EoE himges on a volatile angry young man refusing to take no for an answer. He has a violent side as shown when he tried to hijack Unit 03 to attack his father, and he never subjects Kaworu to the same harassment he gave to Asuka (if only because they had a few days at best). There's a difference, ever so slightly, between the way he treats the men and women in his life. The bad guys win and THE WORLD IS DESTROYED because of misogyny, I know that scared me straight back in high school. Shinji mustn't run away, not just because it's the only way he can save himself and find happiness, but also because that self-loathing can easily turn into violence if left unchecked. It's why I make a distinction between the TV and EoE as seperate endings, because EoE isn't just another finale for the series but a demonstration of where Shinji's path could take if he doesn't learn from his mistakes. I feel the Rebuilds missed an opportunity to explore more of this honestly, with their played completely straight fanservicey aspects interfering with what the original series was trying to do.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Wed Mar 19, 2025 11:27 am OneShotPunch wrote: or his visions of Rei, Misato and Asuka while he was in Unit 01

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Wed Mar 19, 2025 12:00 pm kuribo-04 wrote: OneShotPunch#944689 wrote: or his visions of Rei, Misato and Asuka while he was in Unit 01

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Fri Mar 21, 2025 9:41 pm Ehhh. I don't want to say Shinji has a healthy relationship with, well, anyone, but I don't see why it should be seen quite specifically through the lens of gender. Or at least, we shouldn't apply that lens and assume it objective without considering what lens it is in which Shinji himself operates. There's a question of intent. Yes, a person or action can be misogynistic without the intent of misogyny, but what I mean is we need to separate Shinji's attitude toward Asuka and whether or not it indicates an attitude toward all women. Misogyny is, definitionally, "a cultural attitude of hatred for females because they are female ." You have to answer to that--are Shinji's actions explainable because of some overall view of women, or more specifically just his view of Asuka? We'd need to probe what the cultural gender expression exactly is in the setting, in the first place, and how Shinji has absorbed it ... and, it being a future setting, it seems quite progressive. Many women hold top positions of authority. Asuka is demonstrably the most gifted combat pilot. Who did Shinji learn his supposed anti-women views from? Not from his father, who didn't raise him. His teacher? This framing seems to predicate so much on gender that it, ironically, hamstrings things a bit and reduces things to gender. I just really dislike this idea of narrowing the EoE strangling into "domestic violence" when it's a loaded and layered scene, and it has as much to do with how abusive Shinji usually isn't at the expense of welcoming his own abuse. It's the inversion of and result of a long-standing status quo of non-assertiveness, a series of negative encounters Asuka is responsible for that can be cast as psychological and physical abuse, as she kicks and slaps him multiple times through the series. The EoE strangle has a lot of room for plausible deniability because of how interpretative it is--it's phantasmagoria, so it could indicate many things behind Shinji's motivation. Could he perhaps be testing to see if it's really Asuka, and not a regurgitation or vision? Is it more like mental roleplay? Are Shinji and Asuka standing in for or indirectly referencing something more oblique? I'm not meaning to 'Shinjisplain,' but Shinji thought about strangling whereas Asuka was abusive multiple times irl. Is she misandrist? Or another spin on the question: If Shinji had jerked off over Kaworu, what would we call that? Is there a word for that? Unless Shinji is performing actions and motivated by a gender-based view of things, and thinks about things in a strict, unhealthy gender binary, I don't think gender is a very good lens. It feels more organic to view it as simply character-based, on a human to human level. Shinji's problems are never resolved on a gender binary, after all, it's always about his interactions with other people in general, and it's because that's the core issue--Shinji vs others, not exactly just Shinji vs women.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sat Mar 22, 2025 2:00 am Fortunately, I wasn't saying gender is an objective lens but one lens, a lesn that I feel shouldn't be thrown out of hand when discussing Evangelion. i feel that the show itself invites it when one of the principal characters has a whole speech about men and women being separated by rivers. You bring up women being in high positions of power, but we see in several episodes that this power isn't automatically respected. The Jet Alone episode has Tokida saying the Eva acted like a hysterial woman, much to the laughter of the crowd. It is a source of frustration for Misato and Ritsuko that they're not taken seriously. Meanwhile, Toji and Kensuke have a lucrative business taking pictures of their female classmates and selling them off for profit. And they're supposed to be the LIKABLE friend characters to Shinji. The MAGI is specifically said to be made from Naoko's brainwave patterns, where she separates her roles as a mother, a woman and scientist, suggesting these cultural norms still have those ideas. SEELE humiliate Ritsuko by stripping her naked in their meeting. Shinji masturbates over Asuka. Gendo grabs Rei's breast. I argue that Misogyny is a factor in this setting, and Shinji doesn't really need a particular person to hand these ideals down from when the culture around him seems like a well enough justification for not having the best view of women. Maybe my memory isn't sharp, I can only think of one time where Asuka kicked Shinji, and it was in the comedic dance episode. You could argue that and the verbal insults she throws at Shinji are abusive, maybe. But in all the times Shinji thinks of how people hate him, he never seems to consider any of Asuka's actions as abusive in particular. if anything he's attracted to her assertedness, and latches onto her as a source of comfort since in his mind she's better than him. He does something similar to this with Kaworu, but whereas with Asuka he keeps his distance and his desires are mainly expressed while she's unconscious (... there's got to be a better way to phrase that), Shinji and Kaworu's scenes are those of non-sexual intimacy. They hold hands, they talk about music, they lay in bed. That's why there's no scene of Shinji msaturbating to Kaworu, because that's a relationship meant to contrast Shinji and Asuka brief as it was. If he jerked off over Kaworu's comatose body, that would mean he considers him a sexual object, something that Shinji most certainly did with Asuka in EoE. And I argue even outside of Asuka, there are moments where Shinji does have a bit of a binary view on women. At the end of the Jet Alone episode, he still grumbles over Misato's slobbiness until Kensuke points out Misato is showing that slobbiness 'cause she's acting ike a mother. He makes a comment about Rei acting like a mother himself during an elevator scene, which makes her blush, unaware that the reason Rei acts so submissive is because she was made that way by Gendo. I think Misogny is a factor in both the setting and the characters. Hell, Misato's whole angst comes from seeing herself as a whore despite- as far as we know- she only ever had sex with Kaji in college.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Sat Mar 22, 2025 2:53 am OneShotPunch wrote: i feel that the show itself invites it when one of the principal characters has a whole speech about men and women being separated by rivers.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by nearsecondimpact » Sat Mar 22, 2025 8:50 am I definitely feel like there's some air of misogyny to Shinji in the way that there is for most characters in the show. I would go as far as to say that all of the characters (yes, including the women!) in NGE espouse some form of misogyny whether it be through objectification, infantilisation, the aforementioned typecasting in to rigid gender roles, or in the case of Gendo, outright sexual violence (!!!). As for the women in the show, as mentioned by OneShotPunch, Misato suffers from some form of internalised misogyny in the form of thinking herself promiscuous while only having one named sexual partner. I would extend this by mentioning how Asuka and Rei also display their own forms of internalised misogyny; Asuka in sexualising herself (i.e. her only way of becoming a woman is to have relations with Kaji, and in her mind this is the only way she can enforce her femininity.) and Rei in providing herself as a service to Gendo, viewing herself as lesser (though I think this is related to her gender struggles within the show, mostly within the way she sometimes operates as a foil to Shinji through being someone Gendo can "love" because of his own misogyny... I'll relent you can also very much read this as her clone-ness getting to her, and I think that's honestly an equally valid reading). Misogyny is definitely important to this world, and a really important part of the way characters interact. But, I would argue this is intended as less of a commentary on misogyny in society and more a commentary on the show's themes of isolation and struggling to understand other people. This time, it's just from the lens of gender. While I don't think it serves well to say the character boils down to that of a clear-cut misogynist, I would think it pertinent to view some of the latent misogynistic tendencies that we do see him displaying as an extension of the show's framing of the inherent differences between people. In this case, it happens to be an entire gender class. It presents Shinji's own misogyny as another example of the barriers he puts up between himself and other people, and I would argue as something to be unlearnt. I mean, to me this unlearning has always read as a big part of his instrumentality sequence; maybe not in as many words, but I do think it's there. Asuka even deliberately calls him out on his sexualization of her! Furthermore, I think it's worth noting that just because he is a participant in the show's misogyny... doesn't make him a bad person or badly-written character. You can be a participant in the framework of sexism and not hate women. Misogynistic behaviour alone does not make a misogynist! Look at the difference between Shinji, and, say, Gendo - Shinji sexualises women and will demean them to some extent, but Gendo clearly cares very little about the direct autonomy, desires and safety of the women around him, and goes out of his way to enact violence against them in the way that the larger framework of sexism explicitly allows him to. Misogyny is a learnt behaviour that society tends to enforce, and one we all must actively work to identify and try not to participate in. I would consider myself a feminist, but I'll be happy to admit there will have been times in my life where I have been misogynistic - the way forwards was to think critically about what I had enacted, what experiences lead me to that behaviour, and how I could think in a way that meant I wouldn't do so again! And I think in considering all of this it highlights a very critical way in which I would argue you are supposed to view the men in the show, even if just a little... such that someone like Gendo, who I would argue is very much a misogynist, is the product of drawing inside yourself and refusing to accept or respect other points of view. This is something that is brought up during his Rebuild Instrumentality - which although I feel is super flawed (ESPECIALLY in its treatment of women) and I won't speak about at length, I feel does hammer down this aspect of him. Gendo is what happens when someone like Shinji is left unchecked and allowed to draw further within himself, to the point in which everyone around him - including women, who, due to his position of power and gender are very easy for him to subjugate - become lesser. TL;DR: misogyny in this show appears to be viewed largely as a byproduct of self isolation and the ensuing lack of self-criticality... But that's just my thoughts on it! I'd be happy to talk more about it... like I said, I'm an annoying feminist!!

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Sat Mar 22, 2025 8:46 pm Great post! I have nothing to add except... nearsecondimpact wrote: This is something that is brought up during his Rebuild Instrumentality - which although I feel is super flawed (ESPECIALLY in its treatment of women) and I won't speak about at length, I feel does hammer down this aspect of him.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by nearsecondimpact » Sun Mar 23, 2025 8:01 am This piques my interest! I understand if you'd rather not, but I'd be interested in reading more.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sun Mar 23, 2025 12:48 pm Axx°N N. wrote: But my argument is that this frustration isn't tantamount to misogyny, and that I view it as one part of his overall alienation from society in the broadest sense and thus from all genders. His relationship with men isn't any less complicated, in various directions, and for it to round out as misogyny as I understand it, his relationship with men would have to be warped and over-compensating and pathological as complement to his disavowal of women or his view of them as being inferior. But there's no superiority at play. That's kind of anti-Shinji.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Sun Mar 23, 2025 12:57 pm nearsecondimpact wrote: I’d be more than happy to talk about it, granted it wouldn’t be deemed too off-topic.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sun Mar 23, 2025 1:17 pm Axx°N N. wrote: Now, I don't think this is a result of sexism or intentional on Anno's part. I think it's more like an unfortunate byproduct of the choice to center the Rebuilds so much around Shinji. He's the only one that enough time is spent on so that his vacillations of character are complex; everyone else, man or woman, is obligated to orbit around him so much that their screentime could only factor out into something one-dimensional, and end up, as token of that flatness, unable to escape an unfortunate symbolic reality of women orbiting men.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Thu Apr 03, 2025 8:00 am You might mean Anno's misogyny...

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Thu Apr 03, 2025 10:01 am Javi2541997 wrote: You might mean Anno's misogyny...

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by nearsecondimpact » Sat Apr 05, 2025 4:24 pm Javi2541997 wrote: You might mean Anno's misogyny...

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Sat Apr 05, 2025 5:16 pm nearsecondimpact wrote: Side note, it does kinda bug me when people chalk down all the narrative choices to Anno. Plenty of other people worked on the show and wrote the scripts! (And I do kinda feel like maybe that’s part of the issue with Rebuilds, because he was given so much more control… sometimes they feel more about Anno than they do about depressed teenager giant robot fights.)

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sat Apr 05, 2025 11:34 pm Rebuild's take on the core three women of Eva I think is a good place to start with that. Misato comes out the least scathed, but in the original there was definitely mean to be a contrast between the party girl behavior she exhibited at home, the operations manager at NERV, and the mother figure she tried and failed to be for Shinji and Asuka. Most of that was sanded off. While we had episodes and scenes dedicated to showing how Second Impact and the death of her father affected her and her relationships, the movies hardly go into that and Misato is pretty much designated as a mother figure. A failed one granted, given the situation with Kaji Jr, but nonetheless her dynamic with Shinji is a lot less *confused* than it was in the show, no EoE kiss for example. Arguably you might say that's a good thing, but it shows the movies weren't focused too much on her interiority so much as how she relates to Shinji. Hell, an analysis on this site even suggested that Mari was her "replacement" due to the similarties between Misato's first scene and Mari's last in Thrice, perhaps Mari might have been intended as a sexy dream girlfriend type Misato wasn't capable of being. Rei is harder to pin down her issues since we had two versions of her, but while Shinji was the catalyst for her growth in the original, we don't get as much of her thought process in these movies, how she feels disconnected from other people or incomplete. The main focus of her scenes in the first two Rebuilds was largely about bringing Shinji and Gendo together, before dying. Then Rei Q shows up, and is meant as a more naive character, not knowing what grass is like. Shinji accepting her as a Rei is a part of his arc, but while I like the idea of Q living out the rest of her life working and finding meaning in that, Thrice went about in a stereotypical way that you'd see in a lot of imitator characters in anime. To top it off, there isn't any passing commentary on the way Gendo raised her to be his tool for revenge, or how he stares creepily at her naked, which downplays that sexual trauma. Asuka arguably got the worst treatment. She's introduced as an angry, brash pilot but we see inside to reveal its the reuslt of trauma and tying her self worth into being a pilot. However, in the Rebuilds a lot of that was absent, and worse it was replaced with her being a clone who seemed programmed to like Shinji the moment she saw him. Unlike in the original where Shinji became the one obsessed with her, Shikinami seemed unable to move on from Shinji despite the fourteen years acting as a pilot for WILLE. In fact, she's immature, unable to express her feelings properly like a tyoical tsundere, stubborn to the point of lying about Shinji's actual involvement with Third Impact because she couldn't handle the fact he chose Rei over her essentially. You can chalk up to the Curse of Eva preventing personal growrth, shows that there wasn't a lot of effort to explore what she'd be like as her own person. The ultimate resolution was Asuka and Shinji admitting they had feelings for each other, and being shipped right back to Kensuke "I took pics of your nakeed ass to sell to other horny boys" Aida. I don't want Asuka and Shinji together, but I somehow hate Asuka and Kensuke just as much if not more so. There is no explanation for that beyond forced heterosexuality. Sure, the movie format probably meant characterization wasn't going to be as detailed or complex as the original. But there's something to be said about how these complicated people in the original were molded into more sterotypical characters.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Jimmy 3, People 0 » Mon Apr 28, 2025 1:12 am Shinji is someone who fully expects the world to revolve around him and inevitably lashes out when it doesn't - and, yes, his misogyny plays a crucial role in that. it's honestly malpractice that it's taken this long for this to become an even remotely widespread position amongst fans, but here we are. I've never been the most eloquent when it comes to analysis, but it genuinely blows my mind that people can watch Shinji treat the women in his life as toys, breaking one in a rage when she flatly refuses to play along, and think misogyny doesn't factor into his arc at all. not only that, but so, so many people go as far as giving him the benefit of the doubt, rationalizing his actions at every turn with "well, he's just a kid!" or "well, Asuka was a bitch to him!" - and not for nothing, but Asuka is nowhere close to as bad as she's made out to be by large swathes of the fanbase. the false equivalencies people make between her and Shinji's actions are staggering. OneShotPunch wrote: Asuka arguably got the worst treatment. She's introduced as an angry, brash pilot but we see inside to reveal its the reuslt of trauma and tying her self worth into being a pilot. However, in the Rebuilds a lot of that was absent, and worse it was replaced with her being a clone who seemed programmed to like Shinji the moment she saw him. Unlike in the original where Shinji became the one obsessed with her, Shikinami seemed unable to move on from Shinji despite the fourteen years acting as a pilot for WILLE. In fact, she's immature, unable to express her feelings properly like a tyoical tsundere, stubborn to the point of lying about Shinji's actual involvement with Third Impact because she couldn't handle the fact he chose Rei over her essentially. You can chalk up to the Curse of Eva preventing personal growrth, shows that there wasn't a lot of effort to explore what she'd be like as her own person. The ultimate resolution was Asuka and Shinji admitting they had feelings for each other, and being shipped right back to Kensuke "I took pics of your nakeed ass to sell to other horny boys" Aida. I don't want Asuka and Shinji together, but I somehow hate Asuka and Kensuke just as much if not more so. There is no explanation for that beyond forced heterosexuality.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Mon Apr 28, 2025 8:36 pm Jimmy 3, People 0 wrote: Shinji is someone who fully expects the world to revolve around him and inevitably lashes out when it doesn't - and, yes, his misogyny plays a crucial role in that. it's honestly malpractice that it's taken this long for this to become an even remotely widespread position amongst fans, but here we are.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by Jimmy 3, People 0 » Tue Apr 29, 2025 2:46 am OneShotPunch wrote: The way the movies handled it though, they never felt right to me partly because of how many other characters' stories were sacriiced for it and because- despite paying lipservice to him needing to grow up and not be selfish- most of the actual problems are not caused by his personality, but due to being railroaded into bad decisions by others, particularly Gendo. Compared to the original's feelings of self hate and discovery, the Rebuilds feel like a product of looking back and going "well I did nothing wrong there. Bye!" which didn't sit right.

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
[]: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Tue Mar 18, 2025 6:47 pm I wouldn't call a character who masturbates over a comatose Asuka someone with a healthy respect for women, just throwing it out there. Why is it some people on here and Reddit seem to balk at Shinji being misogynistic or analyzing his behavior through that lens? Even outside of the masturbation scene, we have moments such as Shinji attempting to kiss a sleeping Asuka (showing he disregarded her boundaries longer than EoE), or his visions of Rei, Misato and Asuka while he was in Unit 01 and Instrumentality, or how EoE has Asuka's rejecting him literally cause the end of the world. These moments particularly with how he seems to get angry over Misato having sex with Kaji in either version of Instrumentality, suggest he has a hard time seeing women as people. Sure I feel some Tumblr analysis overemphasize particular moments- his comment in Ep. 1 about Misato being immature in particular is clearly a joke at her expense for stealing batteries- but I wouldn't say it's unwarrented. EoE stages Shinji's attempted murder of Asuka like a domestic abuse scene for a reason, and I think it adds a dimension to his character and the themes of the series. Shinji's biggest problems come from his self-loathing, how he tries to use different things as a replacement for a sense of self, such as being a pilot or winning his father's love, which leads to him not understanding himself or other people. The misogyny, in my opinion, is the ugliest representation of Shinji's self-hate leading to a lack of understanding, it's the way he most resembles a stereotypical anime audience member/nerd/whatever you wanna put in here. He's not a one dimensional woman hater, he has legitimate problems that would bother most people, he tries to help at times despite himself, but in addition to being blind to how people don't immediately hate him- he never tried to reconcile with Toji even though Toji probably wouldn't think he was responsible- he has difficulty reconciling the complexities in the women in his life even though he wants them to be a source of comfort. Misato, dressed as a stereotypical mother figure in that sandbox scene, has a sexual side that angers Shinji for reasons beyond just not wanting

 the complexities in the women in his life even though he wants them to be a source of comfort. Misato, dressed as a stereotypical mother figure in that sandbox scene, has a sexual side that angers Shinji for reasons beyond just not wanting to see her fuck Kaji. The climax of EoE himges on a volatile angry young man refusing to take no for an answer. He has a violent side as shown when he tried to hijack Unit 03 to attack his father, and he never subjects Kaworu to the same harassment he gave to Asuka (if only because they had a few days at best). There's a difference, ever so slightly, between the way he treats the men and women in his life. The bad guys win and THE WORLD IS DESTROYED because of misogyny, I know that scared me straight back in high school. Shinji mustn't run away, not just because it's the only way he can save himself and find happiness, but also because that self-loathing can easily turn into violence if left unchecked. It's why I make a distinction between the TV and EoE as seperate endings, because EoE isn't just another finale for the series but a demonstration of where Shinji's path could take if he doesn't learn from his mistakes. I feel the Rebuilds missed an opportunity to explore more of this honestly, with their played completely straight fanservicey aspects interfering with what the original series was trying to do.

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
 Mar 21, 2025 9:41 pm Ehhh. I don't want to say Shinji has a healthy relationship with, well, anyone, but I don't see why it should be seen quite specifically through the lens of gender. Or at least, we shouldn't apply that lens and assume it objective without considering what lens it is in which Shinji himself operates. There's a question of intent. Yes, a person or action can be misogynistic without the intent of misogyny, but what I mean is we need to separate Shinji's attitude toward Asuka and whether or not it indicates an attitude toward all women. Misogyny is, definitionally, "a cultural attitude of hatred for females because they are female ." You have to answer to that--are Shinji's actions explainable because of some overall view of women, or more specifically just his view of Asuka? We'd need to probe what the cultural gender expression exactly is in the setting, in the first place, and how Shinji has absorbed it ... and, it being a future setting, it seems quite progressive. Many women hold top positions of authority. Asuka is demonstrably the most gifted combat pilot. Who did Shinji learn his supposed anti-women views from? Not from his father, who didn't raise him. His teacher? This framing seems to predicate so much on gender that it, ironically, hamstrings things a bit and reduces things to gender. I just really dislike this idea of narrowing the EoE strangling into "domestic violence" when it's a loaded and layered scene, and it has as much to do with how abusive Shinji usually isn't at the expense of welcoming his own abuse. It's the inversion of and result of a long-standing status quo of non-assertiveness, a series of negative encounters Asuka is responsible for that can be cast as psychological and physical abuse, as she kicks and slaps him multiple times through the series. The EoE strangle has a lot of room for plausible deniability because of how interpretative it is--it's phantasmagoria, so it could indicate many things behind Shinji's motivation. Could he perhaps be testing to see if it's really Asuka, and not a regurgitation or vision? Is it more like mental roleplay? Are Shinji and Asuka standing in for or indirectly referencing something more oblique? I'm not meaning to 'Shinjisplain,' but Shinji thought about strangling whereas As

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
urgitation or vision? Is it more like mental roleplay? Are Shinji and Asuka standing in for or indirectly referencing something more oblique? I'm not meaning to 'Shinjisplain,' but Shinji thought about strangling whereas Asuka was abusive multiple times irl. Is she misandrist? Or another spin on the question: If Shinji had jerked off over Kaworu, what would we call that? Is there a word for that? Unless Shinji is performing actions and motivated by a gender-based view of things, and thinks about things in a strict, unhealthy gender binary, I don't think gender is a very good lens. It feels more organic to view it as simply character-based, on a human to human level. Shinji's problems are never resolved on a gender binary, after all, it's always about his interactions with other people in general, and it's because that's the core issue--Shinji vs others, not exactly just Shinji vs women.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sat Mar 22, 2025 2:00 am Fortunately, I wasn't saying gender is an objective lens but one lens, a lesn that I feel shouldn't be thrown out of hand when discussing Evangelion. i feel that the show itself invites it when one of the principal characters has a whole speech about men and women being separated by rivers. You bring up women being in high positions of power, but we see in several episodes that this power isn't automatically respected. The Jet Alone episode has Tokida saying the Eva acted like a hysterial woman, much to the laughter of the crowd. It is a source of frustration for Misato and Ritsuko that they're not taken seriously. Meanwhile, Toji and Kensuke have a lucrative business taking pictures of their female classmates and selling them off for profit. And they're supposed to be the LIKABLE friend characters to Shinji. The MAGI is specifically said to be made from Naoko's brainwave patterns, where she separates her roles as a mother, a woman and scientist, suggesting these cultural norms still have those ideas. SEELE humiliate Ritsuko by stripping her naked in their meeting. Shinji masturbates over Asuka. Gendo grabs Rei's breast. I argue that Misogyny is a factor in this setting, and Shinji doesn't really need a particular person to hand these ideals down from when

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
 naked in their meeting. Shinji masturbates over Asuka. Gendo grabs Rei's breast. I argue that Misogyny is a factor in this setting, and Shinji doesn't really need a particular person to hand these ideals down from when the culture around him seems like a well enough justification for not having the best view of women. Maybe my memory isn't sharp, I can only think of one time where Asuka kicked Shinji, and it was in the comedic dance episode. You could argue that and the verbal insults she throws at Shinji are abusive, maybe. But in all the times Shinji thinks of how people hate him, he never seems to consider any of Asuka's actions as abusive in particular. if anything he's attracted to her assertedness, and latches onto her as a source of comfort since in his mind she's better than him. He does something similar to this with Kaworu, but whereas with Asuka he keeps his distance and his desires are mainly expressed while she's unconscious (... there's got to be a better way to phrase that), Shinji and Kaworu's scenes are those of non-sexual intimacy. They hold hands, they talk about music, they lay in bed. That's why there's no scene of Shinji msaturbating to Kaworu, because that's a relationship meant to contrast Shinji and Asuka brief as it was. If he jerked off over Kaworu's comatose body, that would mean he considers him a sexual object, something that Shinji most certainly did with Asuka in EoE. And I argue even outside of Asuka, there are moments where Shinji does have a bit of a binary view on women. At the end of the Jet Alone episode, he still grumbles over Misato's slobbiness until Kensuke points out Misato is showing that slobbiness 'cause she's acting ike a mother. He makes a comment about Rei acting like a mother himself during an elevator scene, which makes her blush, unaware that the reason Rei acts so submissive is because she was made that way by Gendo. I think Misogny is a factor in both the setting and the characters. Hell, Misato's whole angst comes from seeing herself as a whore despite- as far as we know- she only ever had sex with Kaji in college.

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by nearsecondimpact » Sat Mar 22, 2025 8:50 am I definitely feel like there's some air of misogyny to Shinji in the way that there is for most characters in the show. I would go as far as to say that all of the characters (yes, including the women!) in NGE espouse some form of misogyny whether it be through objectification, infantilisation, the aforementioned typecasting in to rigid gender roles, or in the case of Gendo, outright sexual violence (!!!). As for the women in the show, as mentioned by OneShotPunch, Misato suffers from some form of internalised misogyny in the form of thinking herself promiscuous while only having one named sexual partner. I would extend this by mentioning how Asuka and Rei also display their own forms of internalised misogyny; Asuka in sexualising herself (i.e. her only way of becoming a woman is to have relations with Kaji, and in her mind this is the only way she can enforce her femininity.) and Rei in providing herself as a service to Gendo, viewing herself as lesser (though I think this is related to her gender struggles within the show, mostly within the way she sometimes operates as a foil to Shinji through being someone Gendo can "love" because of his own misogyny... I'll relent you can also very much read this as her clone-ness getting to her, and I think that's honestly an equally valid reading). Misogyny is definitely important to this world, and a really important part of the way characters interact. But, I would argue this is intended as less of a commentary on misogyny in society and more a commentary on the show's themes of isolation and struggling to understand other people. This time, it's just from the lens of gender. While I don't think it serves well to say the character boils down to that of a clear-cut misogynist, I would

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
 the show's themes of isolation and struggling to understand other people. This time, it's just from the lens of gender. While I don't think it serves well to say the character boils down to that of a clear-cut misogynist, I would think it pertinent to view some of the latent misogynistic tendencies that we do see him displaying as an extension of the show's framing of the inherent differences between people. In this case, it happens to be an entire gender class. It presents Shinji's own misogyny as another example of the barriers he puts up between himself and other people, and I would argue as something to be unlearnt. I mean, to me this unlearning has always read as a big part of his instrumentality sequence; maybe not in as many words, but I do think it's there. Asuka even deliberately calls him out on his sexualization of her! Furthermore, I think it's worth noting that just because he is a participant in the show's misogyny... doesn't make him a bad person or badly-written character. You can be a participant in the framework of sexism and not hate women. Misogynistic behaviour alone does not make a misogynist! Look at the difference between Shinji, and, say, Gendo - Shinji sexualises women and will demean them to some extent, but Gendo clearly cares very little about the direct autonomy, desires and safety of the women around him, and goes out of his way to enact violence against them in the way that the larger framework of sexism explicitly allows him to. Misogyny is a learnt behaviour that society tends to enforce, and one we all must actively work to identify and try not to participate in. I would consider myself a feminist, but I'll be happy to admit there will have been times in my life where I have been misogynistic - the way forwards was to think critically about what I had enacted, what experiences lead me to that behaviour, and how I could think in a way that meant I wouldn't do so again! And I think in considering all of this it highlights a very critical way in which I would argue you are supposed to view the men in the show, even if just a little... such that someone like Gendo, who I would argue is very much a misogynist, is the product of drawing inside yourself and refusing to accept or respect other points of view. This is something that is brought up during his Rebuild Instrumentality - which although I feel is super flawed (ESPECIALLY in

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
 much a misogynist, is the product of drawing inside yourself and refusing to accept or respect other points of view. This is something that is brought up during his Rebuild Instrumentality - which although I feel is super flawed (ESPECIALLY in its treatment of women) and I won't speak about at length, I feel does hammer down this aspect of him. Gendo is what happens when someone like Shinji is left unchecked and allowed to draw further within himself, to the point in which everyone around him - including women, who, due to his position of power and gender are very easy for him to subjugate - become lesser. TL;DR: misogyny in this show appears to be viewed largely as a byproduct of self isolation and the ensuing lack of self-criticality... But that's just my thoughts on it! I'd be happy to talk more about it... like I said, I'm an annoying feminist!!

[]: Re: My perspective on Shinji's misogyny             Quote Post by OneShotPunch » Sat Apr 05, 2025 11:34 pm Rebuild's take on the core three women of Eva I think is a good place to start with that. Misato comes out the least scathed, but in the original there was definitely mean to be a contrast between the party girl behavior she exhibited at home, the operations manager at NERV, and the mother figure she tried and failed to be for Shinji and Asuka. Most of that was sanded off. While we had episodes and scenes dedicated to showing how Second Impact and the death of her father affected her and her relationships, the movies hardly go into that and Misato is pretty much designated as a mother figure. A failed one granted, given the situation with Kaji Jr, but nonetheless her dynamic with Shinji is a lot less *confused* than it was in the show, no EoE kiss for example. Arguably you might say that's a good thing, but it shows the movies weren't focused too much on her interiority so much as how she relates to Shinji. Hell, an analysis on this site even suggested that Mari was her "replacement" due to the similarties between Misato's first scene and Mari's last in Thrice, perhaps Mari might have been intended as a sexy dream girlfriend type Misato wasn't capable of being. Rei is harder to pin down her issues since we had two versions of her, but while Shinji was the catalyst for her growth in the original, we don't get as much of her thought process in these movies, how she feels disconnected from other people or incomplete. The main focus of her scenes in the first two Rebuilds was largely about bringing Shinji and Gendo together, before dying. Then Rei Q shows up, and is meant as a more naive character, not knowing what grass is like. Shinji accepting her as a Rei is a part of

Article: My perspective on Shinjis misogyny
 Rebuilds was largely about bringing Shinji and Gendo together, before dying. Then Rei Q shows up, and is meant as a more naive character, not knowing what grass is like. Shinji accepting her as a Rei is a part of his arc, but while I like the idea of Q living out the rest of her life working and finding meaning in that, Thrice went about in a stereotypical way that you'd see in a lot of imitator characters in anime. To top it off, there isn't any passing commentary on the way Gendo raised her to be his tool for revenge, or how he stares creepily at her naked, which downplays that sexual trauma. Asuka arguably got the worst treatment. She's introduced as an angry, brash pilot but we see inside to reveal its the reuslt of trauma and tying her self worth into being a pilot. However, in the Rebuilds a lot of that was absent, and worse it was replaced with her being a clone who seemed programmed to like Shinji the moment she saw him. Unlike in the original where Shinji became the one obsessed with her, Shikinami seemed unable to move on from Shinji despite the fourteen years acting as a pilot for WILLE. In fact, she's immature, unable to express her feelings properly like a tyoical tsundere, stubborn to the point of lying about Shinji's actual involvement with Third Impact because she couldn't handle the fact he chose Rei over her essentially. You can chalk up to the Curse of Eva preventing personal growrth, shows that there wasn't a lot of effort to explore what she'd be like as her own person. The ultimate resolution was Asuka and Shinji admitting they had feelings for each other, and being shipped right back to Kensuke "I took pics of your nakeed ass to sell to other horny boys" Aida. I don't want Asuka and Shinji together, but I somehow hate Asuka and Kensuke just as much if not more so. There is no explanation for that beyond forced heterosexuality. Sure, the movie format probably meant characterization wasn't going to be as detailed or complex as the original. But there's something to be said about how these complicated people in the original were molded into more sterotypical characters.
